speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Ruth Crane. This is tape seven. Ruth, you were talking about arriving in Blechhammer. Can you describe your first impressions of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first impressions were-- I mean, we were bewildered. We were not used to such a huge camp. And it was utterly chaotic. And somehow it was much more difficult to settle in even, if you can call it that, even than to Seibersdorf.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say utterly chaotic. Can you--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was chaotic because we didn't sort of know what-- you know, you had to-- you had a role as well, you know? You were sort of in a certain mode. And if you had certain work here, whatever presented itself, it was in a different way. We knew that we might be there a few months. We knew that we might be there a few weeks. But we were treated-- again, we were faceless. Of course, our names were our names, but again, you had to readjust to different people. There were all kind of people there come.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were-- there were not only Jews. There were-- there were Poles there, too, some of them, you know? There were all kinds of other people coming in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you really-- again, you lived from second to second. You really-- in a way, your focus was such that it made you oblivious, you know, to other things around you, although I remember all of this and I can visualize it. I can see it. I can see where the washing was done. I can see where I swung, you know, when I go to the upper bunk. I can see it all, but it's not really sort of that it is in a certain procession, that it's in a regular way, like-- like in the first camp, you see. Because, again, it was again a different scene. It was-- you know, so this is how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your sleeping quarters?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an enormous room again. And I didn't have proper-- once we slept here, once we slept there. Once some they told us to shift. They told us to move.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think what it was meant to do is to reduce us further to nothing, you know? That was not the time yet that we had the number, but that was sort of a-- sort of a-- well, in preparation of it, you know? Things like this. So we were-- we were meant to be totally confused. They meant to confuse us totally.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, I mean, all I can tell you is how it felt then, but, you know, I can see it. I can-- I know where it was and I know how it was, but I can't really describe it to you because it was done in such a way to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to make you-- to made you feel or made you lose your feeling, or whatever. But it was certainly-- it was certainly very barbaric, and it was certainly-- there was, too, a plan. There was a plan that was developed to treat you at this stage. It didn't matter if you perished, if you fell. Whatever happened, it didn't matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said the camp was huge.

speaker: interviewee
text: The camp was huge, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you somehow describe the size of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I really-- I was there-- not really to me, coming in, it was very large because people were coming, people were going. The rooms were large. There were quarters.

speaker: interviewer
text: The men's quarters were very large. So there was no way that I could get into the men's quarters, you see? You were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was only taken to where the girls were. I just saw them from far, and I could see it was a vast-- it was vast, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: It was very large. Blechhammer was a camp that was really-- that it's known for this vastness, you know? And because-- you know, it was like-- as I said, it was and coming and going. So it was-- When you were in Blechhammer, were you able to maintain contact with the other girls from Oderberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, somehow, yes. Yes, somehow we did, because, you see, there were more men than there were girls, always. And somehow-- it was bewildered to me, because you could sense that there were romances there as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: You would be surprised, you know, that there were probably some girls who were-- they got crushes on boys, you know? And those that stayed there, those that were there already for many months or for a year or whatever, because it was, you know? They already were sort of like established, you know? So they lived from day to day, but they allowed themselves certain things which for us it was not possible. Did you know anybody who was in a relationship there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. But it looked to me that I think could have been that there were even couples. There were even from before the war young couples. A few couples I think were there who somehow-- they came from different camps, but they finished there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't really know, but it was sort of-- I knew very little, but it was obvious to me that there were certain things which were not in the other camps. Although, even in the first camp, let's say, a girl that would have been older than I was, I know particularly this ruska, you know? And she-- she looked at that young boy all the time, you know? So she maybe had a crush on him. So occasionally it was perhaps out of davka. You know the Yiddish-- the Yiddish word 'davka'? Just because. So you will remember 'davka.' It's a very well-known word in Yiddish. You know, sometimes they tell you, you can't do it, dear. You can't do it. You can't feel it. Then you say davka. Because you say that you can't, I'm going-- not to show you, but I'm going to show to myself to begin with that I can do it, you know? So perhaps dafka. Perhaps the dafka came into it. What-- what time frame was this in? What time of the year, and what year were you in Blechhammer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, look, it was '40-- that was-- what I'm describing to you, it was '43. '43. End of '42 and '43, OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Blechhammer.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Blechhammer? No, in-- in-- in-- including a bit of Oderberg, the end of it, Seibersdorf, and Blechhammer. [INAUDIBLE] I'd say it would have been [INAUDIBLE] in '43. Something like that, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you stay in Blechhammer for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well it would-- two or three months. Not-- not longer than three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you assigned any particular work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had to do whatever we were told in the morning. We were not told. We were taken, and whatever was there to be done, we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: If it was with washing, it was a large-- after all those men, occasionally, you know, they had to take off something and to have it washed, yes? Or the girls or something like this. So there were girls that were doing the washing. There were girls that were doing in the kitchen. There were girls that were-- whatever it was. You had to partly peel because, you know, the soup that was there, whatever meager portion it was, but it had to be cooked, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, you know, occasionally we got a little bit of cheese. No, not really-- occasionally. Or a little bit of margarine. But most of the time it was just, you know-- whoever came-- whoever you happen to have-- if whoever was standing there mixed it through, so you got a bit, you know-- if you got from the bottom of it, pardon the expression, but it was a bit thicker. Otherwise there could have been kohlrabis or a few-- or some rotten potato or whatever it was that they couldn't use anymore. They dished it out to the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any other-- the roll calls that you experienced in other camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: All along the roll calls and the-- the-- the shouting. All along. That was part and parcel of it, you know? You were-- at that stage, I think that was already after years of this kind of a verbal and physical abuse, that you just, you know-- If I could describe-- if I could paint it for you, I could, but you really-- you know, you couldn't. I mean, you were just hanging in there, really, by the skin of your teeth. There was nothing else for you. Why one survived and the other one-- I-- I believe that every survivor's a walking and talking miracle because that's how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience that same feeling from-- the-- the warmth that you received from the other seven women from Oderberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- Did that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --maintain your spirit in Blechhammer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because now it was-- no, by that time I already met other people, you see, I-- And somehow-- I-- I am very lucky this way. I always was. I don't know if I happened to be lucky to that I meet such people, or maybe they recognized something in me that we respond to each other. So I think it was happening to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Put it this way. I didn't have any other choices. And I don't know if this is a conscious, if I selected this consciously, but I knew one thing, that I could look at the ugly things of it. I could look at the dirt, at the filth, at the hunger, at the destruction. Or I could look at the reflection of-- you know? I could look at the face, because at a Jewish face at times I could look, yes? And I could respond to this. So when they say man does not survive by bread alone, there is something in it. I mean, on the very basic thing, obviously without food you don't-- you don't survive. But it had to be more than just food for you to survive. So did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm convinced of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you form some close friendships with other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really, no. I know one-- one is here. Her name was Sala Schwarzberg. But the rest are really-- you know, I don't-- this was just-- you know, like they say a [? ship ?] [? brought ?] [? romance ?] or whatever, and I think this is what-- what you didn't know. But they were disappearing, and I went, and they left, and I left, you see? After a while, I was sent to Peterswaldau again. There was a-- a-- a group of people, selected, only girls, selected in Blechhammer. And perhaps later on, from different because from Auschwitz as well we had-- and we were-- we went-- we were sent to Peterswaldau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like Peters-- yes? Like Peter [INAUDIBLE]. Waldau-- W-A-L-D-A-U.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the journey to Peterswaldau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, it is the same thing. But when we came there, we were taken to this-- I am saying taken, but, of course, it wasn't as simple as that. There was an old, dilapidated castle. And this is where they-- you can say, house, or whatever. There were around, always around 1,500 girls. And that was not established. That was something that was totally unused for a long, long time. And this is-- because it was on the outskirts of Peterswaldau. And this is where we worked there. We were in the ammunition factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the castle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in the castle, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far out?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was far. It was far. It was close to half-an-hour walk probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you walked--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, this is a very famous-- this is a very famous name. This factory is a very famous name. It was under a very famous name.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I really-- right now, I should've inquired about it. I'm sorry. But it was a very famous name--

speaker: interviewer
text: Famous for what?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was famous for the ammunition it produced. So it was-- again, I was picked at random. And right in the beginning, we had a roll call, and they chose a Judeneltester which was a lucky-- not a lucky-- some people say [GERMAN], but it's not. It's Judeneltester. And she was sort of-- they made her responsible for those 1,500 girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Bronka Weinstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this the same lady who--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because she was really in her 20s then, and she was the only one that had matric. And although she told me later on, if wouldn't be that her parents sort of paid the teachers extra to let her matriculate, you know, she doesn't think that she would have be able, you know, to get her matric. Because it was so, you know, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was made [GERMAN]?

speaker: interviewee
text: That name of the factory was really a very-- [? Kohn ?] or [? Cahn, ?] something like that, a very short name. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You're talking about Bronka?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm talking about Bronka, but that name of this factory-- this name where the-- where the-- ammunition was-- was produced.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, again, I was picked at random. And-- and-- Were you, at this camp, given a number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. This is very important. After a few weeks, we were rounded up and there was this-- this ballroom all-- again, you know, it was a very huge room. And we were-- those 1,500 girls-- we had to undress. And when we looked at each other-- I mean, when you saw it all behind you, I mean, it was a horrible-- I can tell you, it's-- again, you can't describe it. It was indescribable the way we looked by that stage. But what awaited us, we didn't know. When we entered this room, there was a circle-- an oblong, not really a circle, but an oblong. Like a circle, but oblong, you know, shape. And we were pushed into the middle of it, and there was sitting the doctors and all kinds of SS and all kinds of-- a lot of-- a big-- a big crowd.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were prodding, and they were probing. They were looking up you-- you arm. And, you know, they were looking at your face, if you have a rash or if anything. And you knew that you can't look at them in the face, you know? You knew it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of this is probably for the reflexes. They were still looking, you know, if you can-- if you still responded or whatever. But if you were still good for work, or if that they thought that you can still last a few weeks, you know, they put you to the left or something like this. I think it was to the left. And this is what I-- this is the way-- this is the way I remember it. And this is when that it hit me when they put me to the place that I still stayed there. And they put on me the number on a piece of string, engraved on a piece of metal, and the number was 26748. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when this happened-- and that was they not they put it not only by force, but it was-- there were all kinds of shapes of heads. And they were only one size of this string. Even that bit of string was too much to give us as well. So it was really you can barely push it through. And now it was here, it was right here-- and I had a thin neck. And it had to be visible at all-- it had to be visible at all times. And then I thought something happened to me. And then I thought surely there is more-- if I'm alive, there must be more to it than just my physical body. I refuse to live-- I talk to myself, I am not just this physical body. Surely I know that I've got a soul and a spirit as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm not going just to go like this, just to think that if I get that bit of bread, or if I get that bit of water, you know, if I this, that I can't, you know? All those thoughts that you had because all that you could focus, it was the work and then it was if you're going to get that bit of bread. I'm going to get it. I'm not going to get it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then you looked if others need you bit of bread to save them. Things like this. So nothing else really, you know, mattered. But I told, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So next to me, the same evening or the following evening, in the evening, I whispered. There was this girl, Renia Silwerschtz next to me. Could you spell her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Silwer-- Silwer-- S-I-L-W-E-R-Schtz, like you say in German, Schtz-- S-C-H-T-Z, probably something like that. And she was next to me, and she was close to maybe five years older than I was. And she was a very intelligent girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to her-- then I said the way-- you know, as she was-- we were sort of two, you know? And there was like three bunks, and that it was just we were very close together because there were 1,500 girls and 150 girls in-- in a room. So, really, you know, she was right next to me. And I said to her how I felt, you know? I said that I am, you know, like come to a stage when you say, I'm not going to take it anymore. So I was all-- I was going along since it happened with my [NON-ENGLISH]. I was going along told whatever I was to do, yes? But suddenly I thought, no whatever I-- that was it, you know? I felt it's just not enough. It's just not enough that. And we started, and this encouraged her, and her response was terrific because she read what I read, and she knew what I was talking about because she was that much older and her response was such. So it meant that during the day, we went to pieces, you know, whatever allowed us, you know? We were some in bits and pieces. We were on this work, and we were, let's say whatever I did in that factory had to be done again in the minutest detail. And that was horribly hard work because I was just presented-- I was presented-- I was given, you know, those disks, metal disks, and I had to bore holes into them. And that was the beginning of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the holes had to be so accurate in the minutest detail. If I would have made a mistake, it would have meant treason, and it would have meant again punishment, you know, collective punishment. So I-- So this is the way I worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the beginning, I thought when I was asked if I did focus, I thought I did focus. Focus means if you are-- if you are nurtured. If I was-- most of the time, you know, we just barely to-- to survive. But all I did, it was focus. But at night, that few minutes we whispered to one another, and we talked about and in detail, about how Homer, how-- And I realized then that-- that the inspiration I-- I-- if I am alive, I need, in whatever way, that bit of enrichment and inspiration has to come. So, again, you-- you reach deep within you, and you responded to somebody next to you. And we-- and you know what she said to me? If you want me to sing this to you, I will sing it to you. She said to me, Rutka-- [? Rutusch, ?] she said, [? Rutusch, ?] I know that you-- you know, you remember still, as if you-- you were a child, that was a few years ago. But you remember? Let's be children. Do you remember the French songs that you were taught that you-- I said, yes I do. So she said, sing them to me. So I said, how can sing them to you? I mean, you know we're not allowed to sing here. But she said, lean in my ear. So I remember for the first time, she was on the left side, and I leaned forward, and I remember this going this way, you know? The feel of that number on my skin, and I sang into her ear two French songs. And from then on, I had to sing every night to her. What were the two French songs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Want me to sing them to you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SINGING IN FRENCH]

speaker: interviewer
text: And so it went on. It's not the end of it, but, you know. So I-- in the whisper, and so I sang to her in sort of very gently. What it did to me was like an explosion, you know? It meant that I actually used my voice for singing. You know, it's just-- it's indescribable what a human being can-- how much is there within us if we acknowledge this. And, you know? And that's how it was. From then on, through the last minute, through the last minute. I was there for between 13 and 14 months. In this camp, really, you know, we-- again, it was like in Oldenburg. This was-- those two camps was the longest, you know? What effect did this-- this explosion-- when you sang the songs, what effect did it have on you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was acknowledging my being. It-- it-- I was a very young, again. But I-- I sort of-- I-- I-- Like-- like in the-- like in Oldenburg, my body protested, you know, that my body needed to cleanse itself out because it locked itself out for self-preservation. Apparently, you know, you needed-- you just-- you just-- you know, they can take everything away. That's what we whispered to each other. They can take everything away from us, bu they can't take our memories. They can't take our childhood. They can't take it. It is ours. And we were repeating it, again and again. And this girl has survived, and she lives in Israel. And I have seen her. When it was 1990 I saw her in the beginning when I was in Israel years and years ago. But then she was-- her father survived, and she went through a big upheaval, to a big trauma after the war, very traumatic time. She was older than I was, I suppose, you know. She had during the war a boyfriend, as a matter of fact.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so she was telling me about this. She was suddenly-- she was telling me about love, you know. How it felt, you know, to be in love, you know? It was, you know, it was like reading a book. And she was a very well spoken, you know? Very, very well spoken. Very much aware of the power of words, you know? And it really sort of added some sparkle, added something, you know? So you were in-- you said you were in Peterswaldau for about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, well over a year. Well over a year, yes. And this is where I was liberated, in Peterswaldau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember which unit?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember which division--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --military, infantry?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I don't think so that we really remember this. I don't think so because it was, again, it was so chaotic. We remembered it then. Those that wrote it down, they probably would. I can ask someone.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I don't think so that she would remember. You see, because it was-- we didn't know. We didn't know. We didn't know that it's end of the war. We're going to go on the death march, you know? It was-- we heard bits and pieces here and there. There was a [GERMAN] where they sent-- sent some young people-- What's a-- what's a [GERMAN]?

speaker: interviewee
text: A [GERMAN] means it was a room where they kept the-- the sick people. But, you know, again you didn't want to go in the [GERMAN] because you knew that you were-- that you might disappear. And then we had those-- suddenly we had those Hungarian girls who came from Auschwitz. And they came with shaven heads, you know? They was a-- they were just looked even worse than we did, you know? And you had to the last minute, to the day before, you went-- we went away day before we went to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the date of liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Eight of May. Eight of May 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened on that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: On that day, we didn't hear-- in the morning, we didn't hear the sounds that, as horrible as they were, we were already familiar with. So sometimes we just didn't know. We just-- there was nobody there. There was nothing. We just couldn't understand. At least, I didn't know. I couldn't understand. They just all up and left. They all run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: I, myself, if somebody said to me, look, you never know. We might survive. This-- on this one thing, I kept answering the same way. Look, I know the mentality. I-- I hope that you are right. When somebody said something to me like this, I said, you know, I think would they want any witnesses really to what has happened? So to me, anyhow, at certain moments, if I responded, I could respond in a positive way, you know? But then I wanted-- you wanted to fool yourself a bit too, you know? But then there were moments where you felt, you know, that you-- you can fool yourself just that much. But, to me, it seemed that this is occasionally, that this is-- it seemed occasionally, that if somebody said something like this it was hard for me to believe. So sometimes I responded in a certain way. And I said, I think that they want any witnesses if I know their mentality?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, so I was listening to this, and that wasn't very good, but I was listening to this. So when suddenly there was nothing, I just couldn't believe it. I just couldn't believe it. I was all off 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, I mean, a few hours later, we just sat there. We just sat together. We huddled. We-- we-- we-- we sat. We didn't-- we didn't want to move. We were afraid. Some of them were maybe running out, but I remember the sitting, you know, and the waiting, you know. We thought it's something-- before something happens, you know? So the Russians hadn't arrived at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were sitting.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were sitting, no, because they ran before. There was nobody there. In the morning, early in the morning, there was nobody there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a little while later, when we realized that nobody was there, we had bought-- we had the young inmates coming from Regensburg, from other boys coming inside. And guess what? Within 48 hours, a lot of them, who were older than I was, they just paired off. They what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Paired off. And till today, they got married later on, sometimes they don't-- somebody says that they-- they got married first, you know? They just paired off and they became a couple. And, you know, they went away. Some of them a few days later went to Poland, and some of them went to Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: People went in all directions, some of them already in pairs, you know? And somebody like myself, I mean, that was further away from my-- from my thoughts, you know? But that's how it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think because time was so precious, I think that what happened, that in the next 48 hours, probably a lot have got sorted out like they realize that they-- that only, consciously or not, that in order-- they need to create the family, or something like this. And it happened immediately.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ruth, we're going to take a break and change tapes.
